text: So here's the list of steps for a middle lobectomy, the way I do it.
I've chosen 11 steps, which of course is completely arbitrary.
But like the other videos, the first sequence of steps, in this case the first five steps, are always the same for every middle lobectomy.
And then the last sequence of steps, in this case the last six steps,
depend on the patient's anatomy and essentially can be done in any order, depending on whether you have a thick fissure or no fissure.
But just in summary, the sequence of steps, the first is to lift the lung up and divide the inferior pulmonary ligament and get my station 9-8 lymph nodes.
Then I reflect the lung anterior and do the posterior hilar dissection, get my station 7 or subcarinal nodes.
And then I reflect the lung inferior, or caudad, and do my superior hilar dissection, get my paratracheal, stations twos and fours.
Then the lung is reflected posterior.
I dissect the anterior hilum, get a station
10R, and then do a really clean dissection of the right middle lobe vein, separate it from the right upper lobe vein.
And then the next sequence of steps is all arbitrary depending on the fissure.
If it's an ideal case, I'll be able to unroof the pulmonary artery at the confluence of the fissures, and then divide the anterior major fissure

and then divide the right middle lobe vein, and just posterior to the middle lobe vein should be the right middle lobe bronchus, which is divided, and just posterior to that should be the right middle lobe artery, or sometimes there's more than one artery, and those are divided.
And then lastly, I'll divide the minor fissure, thus detaching the right middle lobe.
So let's look at the lobectomy.
Again, the entire procedure is included in this video.
And I speed it up and slow it down to make it 18 minutes.
But essentially, the first step is lift the lung up.
And it's not really a lifting as much as a pushing.
and I divide the inferior pulmonary ligament.
I do this in every lobectomy.
It's a nice warm-up.
You get your station 9 and 8 lymph nodes.
Sometimes you can see the 8, sometimes not.
This is a station 9 lymph node, inferior pulmonary ligament lymph node, a tiny one.
And again, you see I have two spondyloles in the chest.
I have one that I use for sort of blunt dissection and sort of sweeping, and the second one I use with my fourth arm to kind of push the lung.
It's a very atraumatic way to get the lung retracted.
Incidentally, the person who was assisting me with this case had never done a robotic thoracic case before.
She had actually assisted on a bunch of other robotic cases, but I set up the case so that basically anyone can help me.
Here, I'm reflecting the lung anterior and I'm doing my posterior hilar dissection.
This is a station seven or subcarinal lymph node that I'm dissecting out in the subcarinal space.
Again, this is the bloodiest lymph node in the chest.
See, it's got a rich vascular supply.
There's a bunch of little blood vessels.
So I use the bipolar cautery on eight.
It's always on eight, which is the highest setting.
I think you get the best hemostasis.
There's very little thermal spread.
All the energy stays between the tips.
And again, I'm pushing the lung with my fourth arm, the superior segment.
And here I'm just packing some Surgicil in the subcranial space.
It was kind of bloody.
I finish off the posterior hilar dissection.
I don't have to get that station 11 or sump node, which I make a big deal of in the right upper lobe and even the right lower lobe.
But in the middle lobe, it's not as important.
And then I start doing my superior hyaluronidase section.
So you can see the lung is reflected caudate or inferior.
And now I'm doing the whole station 2R slash 4R lymph node dissection.
And I'm taking out as a nodal packet with all the mediastinal fat.
Basically get all of it between, all the nodes between the
superior vena cava and the trachea.
There you're starting to see the anterior border of the trachea.
So I know I'm getting all the paratracheal lymph nodes here.
And it's nice to do this part of the lymph node dissection in the midst of the case rather than waiting until the end of the case.
As soon as the lobe's out, you can essentially just close.
I'll pack the paratracheal lymph node dissection with Surgicel.
Now I will reflect along posterior and do the anterior highlighted section.
This patient had a little adhesion here between the middle lobe tumor and the pericardial fat.
Now the lung is reflected posterior.
And this is kind of the position that VAT surgeons are comfortable with.
I'm using the bipolar cautery to dissect the hilum away from the right phrenic nerve.
And there's a right hilar lymph node, or station 10R, right above the superior pulmonary vein.
So this is my anterior hilar dissection.
And then the next step, which is the last step that's consistent with every middle lobectomy, is I'm going to isolate the middle lobe vein.
This is moving kind of fast now, I know, I realize.

and I wanted to get through it for the viewer.
This is a station 11 lymph node there, and this patient actually had an anomalous venous drainage.
He had the vein to the right middle lobe medial segment was draining into the
inferior pulmonary vein and the lateral segment of the middle lobe is draining into the superior pulmonary vein.
Usually, all the venous drainage of the middle lobe is to the superior pulmonary vein, but this one was a little bit anomalous.
I hadn't quite realized that yet, so here I'm dissecting out the lateral segmental vein for the right middle lobe.
and I'm going to mark it with a vessel loop.

And then, as I state in the intro, the next sequence of steps are based on the anatomy and can be essentially in any sequence.
So the first thing I always do is I try and unroof the...
the pulmonary artery at the confluence of the major and minor fissures.
This patient had a fairly thick
fissure, fairly incomplete.
So I sort of had to dig through some parenchyma to find the pulmonary artery.
And I apologize to the viewer.
I know this is moving kind of fast.
But I felt like I had a plane.
I could sort of see the plane.
I was digging out some station 12 or intraparenchymal lymph nodes here to get to the intralobar pulmonary artery.
And there was actually a small pulmonary artery branch going to the lateral segment of the right middle lobe, or segmental artery A4.
Once I saw I had it, I thought it would be a good idea to dissect it out and divide it.

And here you can see the, it's just a small branch, but it's going to the lateral segment of the middle lobe, and I'm underneath there, and I use these endolead catheters to come underneath and divide all of the vascular structures and even the bronchus.
I use the angle tip stapler.
I always use a gray load for the pulmonary artery branches and most of the vein branches.
I think it's a little bit more hemostatic.
And again, I have a video on my website where I show how I make these little endolyd catheters, which I think are incredibly useful.
So this is division of that A4 segmental branch.
So now I want to divide the anterior aspect of the major fissure.
This patient had thick fissure, which made the middle lobectomy sort of challenging.
Also anomalous venous drainage.
So here you see the medial aspect of the middle lobe draining into the inferior pulmonary vein rather than the superior pulmonary vein.
So this would be segmental vein V5.
And it's draining into the inferior pulmonary vein.
So I'm isolating.
that segmental vein.
which will divide before I divide the anterior part of the fissure.

Normally, I like to get the fissure divided before I divide the vein, but in this case, just the way it's set up and the fissure was so thick, I thought it would be easier to divide this little anomalous branch of the middle vein prior to dividing the fissure.
You can see the angle here.

the posterior to anterior angle of my posterior staple port is going to be used to divide the pulmonary vein, which is usually the way it works out best and is the safest way to divide the middle of vein.
And again, I'm using a gray load, and we're coming across the medial segmental branch of the middle of vein going into the anterior pulmonary artery.
Then again, I look and I want to divide the anterior aspect of the major fissure.
Now that that segmental vein is out of the way, it looks like I'll have a better plane to be able to divide the fissure.
Here I'm using a white load.
Again, on the parenchyma, you don't always need a blue load or a green load.
I use a white load if it looks like it's sort of thin parenchyma.
It looks like I can develop a plane here between the medial hilum and the interlobar pulmonary artery.
And I believe here I actually swapped out my dominant hand, my right hand, from a bipolar instrument to a second tip-up.
I always keep a second tip-up in the tray because it's a long instrument and it's got that great sweeping curve.
It's very blunt and atraumatic.
And I use this to get around big structures like, in this case, the medial aspect of the fissure.
I can get around a large pulmonary vein or even a bronchus.
And then here, I use the larger of the two endolyd catheters that I use for the straight stapler.
It's in the plane, and that's the rest of the anterior fissure.
And I use a blue straight 45.
And you can see the endolyd catheter protects everything in the landing zone, on the other side of the staple, which, of course, is the pulmonary artery in this case.
It's just a very safe way to find that staple.
So now the entire anterior portion of the major fissure is divided.
And here we're seeing the middle of bronchus.
This is little peribronchial or station 11-R lymph node just cleaning off the middle of bronchus, which we see here.
So the middle lobe bronchus is essentially isolated now, and I used the endolyd catheter to get behind it.
I was trying to decide whether to use a green or a blue load in this.
I think you probably could have gone either way.
It was a small, fairly small middle lobe bronchus, so I used the blue load angled tip, which is coming across the middle lobe bronchus here, which is divided.
And then, of course, behind the middle lobe bronchus should be the
middle of artery.
And I think just to get it a little bit more on a pedicle at this point, I decided I would start dividing the minor fissure.
which I do in a lot of cases.
It just basically puts the middle lobe more on a pedicle or a stalk so you can get at the artery.
The fissure was, again, incomplete, and I'm using blue loads to kind of come across right in that plane between the upper lobe and the middle lobe, the minor fissure.
And then I just lift the middle lobe up, and we'll see the main
right middle of artery.
Remember, there was a small accessory middle of artery going to the lateral segment.
This is going to be the larger branch, the larger middle of artery branch that I'm separating from the superior pulmonary vein.
And get the endolyd catheter behind it.
This is the main blood supply to the middle lobe.
What's interesting is once this is divided, you'll see there's another branch of the pulmonary vein.
So this is the pulmonary artery, A4 and 5.
And I'm using a gray angled tip at the endolute catheter.
Once this structure is divided,
Normally, the whole venous supply should be divided at this point, but this patient had a little extra vein branch, and this was the vein draining the lateral segment of the right middle lobe.
So it's kind of corresponding to that little accessory pulmonary artery branch that we divided at the beginning of the case.
This is an extra venous drainage or V4 branch in the lateral segment of the right middle lobe.
And we divide this, and now all the blood supply should be divided.
And I just sort of lift up the middle lobe, and I complete the division of the fissure.
In this case, I used a green lobe because the fissure gets really thick in the middle here.
In fact, it took us two additional staple firings to completely detach the middle lobe.
In this case, the fissure was so thick.
The consult time on this case was between an hour and 50 and an hour and 55 minutes, which is longer than it normally takes us, quite frankly.
But the patient had anomalous venous drainage and a very thick fissure.
And overall, it was a smooth operation, safe.
I was pleased with how it went.
The patient went home on post-operative day number two, like most of our patients do.
And once the middle lobe is detached, the lobe is placed in the ripstop nylon bag and removed to the assistant port.
So that's right middle lobectomy in 11 steps in 18 minutes.
Thanks very much for watching.
